problem text: Imagine you possess a rope with a length of $1$ meter. You randomly make a cut along the rope's length. Determine the variance of the length of the longer segment (in meters).
problem solution: Let $X$ be the distance from the left end where the cut is made. Then $X \sim \text{Unif}(0,1)$ and $L = \text{max}\{X,1-X\}$ represents the length of the longer segment, as the cut at position $X$ results in two segments of lengths $X$ and $1-X$. We know that $L \geq 0.5$, since one of the segments must be at least $1/2$ in length. However, the event $\{L = c\}$ corresponds to $X = c$ or $X = 1-c$, leading to $L \sim \text{Unif}(0.5,1)$, which means $\text{Var}(L) = \dfrac{(0.5)^2}{12} = \dfrac{1}{48}$.
problem hint: Let $X$ be the distance from the left end where the rope is cut. Then $X \sim \text{Unif}(0,1)$ and $L = \text{max}\{X,1-X\}$ represents the length of the longer segment, as the cut at position $X$ results in two segments of lengths $X$ and $1-X$. Try to determine the distribution of $L$.
